results.title: Tessl: Optimize agents skills, ship 3× better code. | Product Hunt
results.content:
@danilpond Two evaluation methods today.   
  
First, skill reviews - when you submit a skill, it gets scored against structure and best practice criteria established by Anthropic, combining validation checks with LLM-judged quality. This tells you immediately whether your skill is well-constructed.   
  
Second, task-based evaluations - scenario-based evals where you run end-to-end tasks and track results against real agent behavior. Teams submit a skill, see their scores, iterate, and resubmit - and we can measure the delta between versions. That second approach is where we validate evaluation scenarios. [...] Agentmemory

Baptiste Fernandez
Tessl

Tessl

@rohit\_ghumare Great to hear! What’s one thing you wish the eval workflow did better - debugging failures, comparing versions, etc? We’re iterating fast based on comments like yours. :)

Rohit Ghumare
Agentmemory

Agentmemory

Alan Pope
Tessl

Tessl

@rohit\_ghumare Hey Rohit! I ran all your skills through the Tessl review machinery and sent you a pull request.

Baptiste Fernandez
Tessl

Tessl [...] Without AI evaluations, you can’t tell if a skill helps, provides minimal uplift or even degrades functionality. You spend your time course-correcting agents instead of shipping.   
  
Tessl is a development platform and package manager for agent skills. With Tessl, we were able to evaluate and optimize ElevenLabs' skills, 2x'ing their agent success in using their APIs.   
  
If you are building a personal project, maintaining an OSS library, or developing with AI at work, you can now evaluate your skill and optimize it to help agents use it properly.  
  
What skills are you working on, and what's your use case for them?

fmerian
Tabstack by Mozilla

Tabstack by Mozilla

I'm an absolute fan of @guypod and the @Tessl team.

results.title: Agent Skills: Find skills for Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot & more | Product Hunt
results.content:
Romain Simon
Melies

Melies

@slavaakulov Those are two excellent points.  
  
Curating the best skills  
Currently all skills are added but they are ranked depending on their security score and usage (github stars, etc).  
However I built in an auto-review mechanism that will hopefully help surface the best skills : if you use the /learn command to install skills, it allows agents to report back if a skill has been useful once used for the first time.  
  
Skill Versionning   
You cannot pin down a specific version, I need to implement that. However, it currently tracks which version of the skill is installed via a content sha. When the skill is used, it should ask you if you want to update to the latest version if you don't have the latest one.

Elvis Bueno

Congrats on the launch! 🚀 [...] So I built agentskill.sh that currently indexes 100k+ skills for Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Windsurf and more; I focused on two things:

Security: Every skill gets scanned across 12 threat categories so you know what you're installing before you install it. You can check the details here: Security Dashboard

Discovery: You can search skills by categories using many criteria, review them, and more.

The fastest way to try it is the /learn command. Once installed you (or your agent) can search and install skills directly using:

`/learn # just find skills for current codebase`
`/learn seo # search by keyword`
`/learn @owner/name # install a specific skill`
`/learn trending # see what's popular`

Using /learn to find skills has another big advantage: it lets your agent learn by itself. [...] Then there is another local scan : if you use the /learn skill to install, the agent is instructed to either run a local automated scanner (if one is available) or read the entire skill before installing it, following the instructions defined in the embedded SECURITY.md of the install skill. If something suspicious is detected, the agent can report it back to the registry so the skill can be reviewed and removed, even if it passed the initial static scan.

Adithya Shreshti
Indie.Deals

Indie.Deals

The URL ends up in a 404, you may want fix the URL as it contains "@"  
  

Romain Simon
Melies

Melies

@adithya Thanks! it's fixed. The url with the ref was weirdly detected as a skill url

Lev Kerzhner
AutonomyAI

results.title: Google Chrome Skills: Turn your best AI prompts into one-click tools in Chrome | Product Hunt
results.content:
### Finance

### AI Agents

### Trending categories

### Top reviewed

### Trending products

### Top forum threads

Wispr Flow: Dictation That Works Everywhere

What do you think? …

Rohan Chaubey

Skills in Chrome is a new feature that turns your best AI prompts into reusable, one-click workflows right inside the browser.

The problem: repeating the same prompts across tabs and tasks is tedious and inefficient.

The solution: save prompts as “Skills” and instantly run them across pages or multiple tabs.

What makes it interesting is the ability to remix, reuse, and execute prompts contextually on live webpages, not just in chat.

Key features:

Save prompts as reusable Skills from chat history

Run Skills across current or multiple tabs

Built-in library of ready-to-use workflows [...] RiteKit Company Logo API

Congrats on the launch! This is a clever way to reduce friction between prompting and execution. I'm curious about the remix feature - when users customize existing Skills, does the tool help them understand what variables or parameters they're changing, or is it more of a free-form editing experience? Wondering how you're making it accessible for non-technical users.

Dontell Levesque

I love this idea, I've been manually reusing prompts forever and this feels like a huge save of time for me already.

Shubham Garg

Is there a way a website can create custom skills for interacting with there website?

Augustin Jerebic
